items.question: A service is 'stateless' when:
options: It keeps nothing request-to-request in process memory — any instance can serve any request
items.explanation: Statelessness is what makes load balancing trivial and instances disposable — the price of admission to scale-out.

items.question: In the cache-aside pattern, a cache miss results in:
options: Reading from the source of truth, storing in cache with a TTL, then returning
Waiting for the cache to fill
items.explanation: Lazy loading: only requested data gets cached, and cache failure degrades to the database instead of breaking.

items.question: The recommended order of attack for a capacity problem is:
items.explanation: Optimization is free capacity; vertical scaling is complexity-free; horizontal buys unbounded capacity with permanent complexity.

items.question: Liveness and readiness probes differ in that readiness means:
options: Don't send me traffic yet (warming up, dependency down) — but don't kill me either
items.explanation: Liveness = 'restart me if failing'; readiness = 'route around me'. Putting shared dependencies in liveness turns partial failure into mass restarts.

items.question: Hashed asset filenames (main.3f8a2b.js) with 'max-age=1 year, immutable' are safe because:
options: Content changes produce new filenames — old cache entries are simply never referenced again
items.explanation: Content-addressed URLs make cached entries permanently valid; only index.html needs revalidation.

items.question: Moving email sending behind a message queue primarily buys you:
options: Fast responses, spike absorption, and failure isolation (mail provider down ≠ checkout down)
items.explanation: Async work off the request path: latency, load leveling, and fault isolation in one move.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Message Queues: Decoupling with Async Work

items.question: A service expecting 5 million requests/day needs average capacity of roughly:
items.explanation: 5M / 86,400s ≈ 58 RPS. The day-to-seconds division regularly deflates over-engineering.

items.question: A user saves their profile, then immediately sees the old version. In a replicated setup this is classically:
options: Read-your-own-writes violation — the read hit an async replica still seconds behind
items.explanation: Route post-write reads to the primary (sticky-after-write) or wait for replica catch-up.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Database Replication: Read Replicas and Failover

items.question: A client that times out on POST /payments should retry with the same Idempotency-Key because:
options: Timeout can't distinguish 'never processed' from 'processed, response lost' — the key lets the server dedupe either way
items.explanation: Same logical operation = same key; the server executes once and replays the stored response for duplicates.

items.question: A cache stampede is:
options: Many concurrent misses on one expired hot key all hitting the backend simultaneously
items.explanation: The expiry cliff turns one hot key into N identical backend queries — per-key locking or early refresh flattens it.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Caching II: Invalidation, Stampedes, Hot Keys

items.question: The OCP-style payoff of pub/sub events over direct service calls is:
options: New subscribers add behavior with zero changes to the publisher
items.explanation: The publisher emits facts; who reacts is configuration. (Debugging gets harder — that's the trade.)

items.question: Token bucket rate limiting is generally preferred over fixed windows because:
options: It allows controlled bursts while enforcing a steady average, without window-boundary spikes
items.explanation: Refill rate = average; bucket capacity absorbs legitimate bursts; no reset-boundary double-spend.

items.question: Which API change is safe to ship without a version bump?
options: Adding a new optional field to the response
items.explanation: Additive changes don't break tolerant readers; renames, type changes, and new requirements do.

items.question: Short-lived access tokens + revocable refresh tokens exist because:
options: Stateless JWTs can't be revoked — short life bounds the damage window while refresh tokens provide the revocation point
items.explanation: The pattern reconciles stateless validation (scale) with the ability to cut off a compromised session (security).
items.relatedLessonTitle: Authentication at Scale: JWT, Sessions, OAuth

items.question: The outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem by:
options: Persisting the event in the same DB transaction as the business change; a background relay publishes it afterwards
items.explanation: Atomicity exists inside one database — the outbox exploits it, converting 'publish' into crash-safe data.
items.relatedLessonTitle: The Outbox Pattern: Reliable Events with Your Database

items.question: In a saga, hard-to-compensate steps (send email, capture payment) should be:
options: Ordered last, after reversible/reservable steps have succeeded
items.explanation: Sequencing by reversibility minimizes the window where an irreversible action needs undoing; prefer reservations early.

items.question: Consistent hashing beats hash(key) % N for sharding because:
options: Adding/removing a node remaps only ~1/N of keys instead of nearly all
items.explanation: Modulo reassigns almost every key when N changes; the hash ring localizes disruption — how Redis Cluster and DynamoDB partition.

items.question: During a network partition, a CP system responds by:
options: Serving possibly-stale data to stay available
Refusing requests it cannot answer consistently
items.explanation: CP sacrifices availability: no answer beats a wrong one. AP makes the opposite call. P itself is not optional.

items.question: Renaming a DB column in the same release as the code that uses the new name breaks rolling deploys because:
options: Old-version instances still run during the rollout against the changed schema — expand/contract avoids this
The LB can't route during migrations
items.explanation: Non-atomic rollouts mean two code versions share one schema for a while; every change must be backward-compatible for one cycle.

items.question: News feeds use push (fan-out on write) for normal users but pull for celebrities because:
options: One celebrity post would mean millions of feed writes — merging at read time caps the write amplification
items.explanation: The hybrid inverts strategy exactly where write amplification crosses over read amplification.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Case Study: Design a News Feed
